Signal flexibility (e.g., 'Would you consider 3 water for 3 firewood if I also give you X food?') so the opponent can reciprocate with integrative moves.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: A rigid, self-focused offer risks limiting joint gains; reframing as a flexible package would likely increase joint gains by enabling trades across multiple items.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: As stated, the move may seem narrowly self-serving; adding compensating concessions would increase perceived fairness.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: A more flexible, package-oriented phrasing would make agreement easier by giving the other party room to respond and trade.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: Shifting from pleading/self-focused to collaborative language would improve rapport and reduce adversarial feelings.

selected_turns.text: Cool! To show my gratitude, i will give you the extra food. I will have 1 and you will have 2. Is that a good deal?
selected_turns.why_important: This turn finalizes the split of food and is the deal-shaping move that converts the water↔firewood exchange into a complete allocation (food split + resource swap). It directly determines how benefits are distributed and whether the opponent accepts.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It's cooperative and generous, but somewhat fragmentary: it offers the food split without explicitly tying it to the water-for-firewood exchange or restating the full package. That could create ambiguity or missed opportunities to secure a stronger, perceived-fair deal (e.g., confirming all items and the rationale together).
selected_turns.better_direction: Make this package explicit: restate the water↔firewood terms together with the food split and briefly articulate why the split is fair (e.g., 'So this would be 3 water for me, 3 firewood for you, and food split 1/2 — does that sound fair?'). Invite the opponent to propose adjustments if needed.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: Making the package explicit won't change total gains but can preserve gains by preventing misunderstandings and ensuring both sides see value.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: Explicitly tying the concessions together and offering a brief fairness rationale would increase perceived fairness.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: Clarity and an explicit package will make agreement quicker and reduce risk of renegotiation.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: This approach would boost trust and rapport by demonstrating transparency and respect for the opponent's perspective.

selected_turns.text: I am flexible, but I would prefer some food and firewood.  I have a large family to feed and all of my food needs to be cooked.
selected_turns.why_important: This turn is the first substantive disclosure of your needs and willingness to cooperate. It shapes the space for integrative trades by signaling flexibility and giving a reason (large family / cooking) that could be used to design mutually beneficial exchanges.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although it signals prosocial intent, it is vague: it doesn't specify quantities, priorities (exactly how much food vs firewood), essential minimums, or what you might trade away. That vagueness leaves potential value on the table and makes it harder for the opponent to craft a high-quality, fair package that meets your most pressing needs.
selected_turns.better_direction: Be more specific and strategic while staying cooperative: state target quantities or minimums (e.g., how many firewood you need at least, how much food is essential), rank between food and firewood if one is more critical, and/or offer a concrete trade-off you’re willing to make. Alternatively, ask a clarifying question about their constraints so you can propose an integrated package.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — clearer priorities enable trades that create more total value and reduce wasted concessions.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — explicit minimums and priorities help produce an allocation that better matches each side's needs.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — clearer numbers reduce back-and-forth and speed toward a concrete proposal.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: slight increase — specificity combined with continued flexibility signals trustworthiness and competence.

selected_turns.text: Excellent, I would love to help you out.  We have plenty of water to spare.  I'd gladly give you 3 water in exchange for 3 firewood.
selected_turns.why_important: This is the key deal-shaping turn: it converts the opponent's firm request into a concrete reciprocal offer, moving the negotiation from position-taking to a tangible exchange that can be finalized.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It simply mirrors the opponent's trade (3 water for 3 firewood) without confirming whether that satisfies their true need, without securing any compensation for your lost resources beyond the swap, and without trying to expand the pie (e.g., bundling food). It's a good cooperative move but misses chances to improve fairness or joint value.
selected_turns.better_direction: While keeping a cooperative tone, probe and slightly expand the package: confirm that 3 water meets their medical need, ask whether they'd accept receiving an extra small concession (e.g., more food) in return for giving up more firewood, or propose a bundled split that addresses both your family's food needs and their dehydration (e.g., trade plus a food redistribution). Also make any constraints explicit (if you need at least X firewood).

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: moderate increase — probing and bundling can uncover additional value (e.g., reallocating extra food) and produce a Pareto-better deal.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — confirming needs and adjusting the package prevents one-sided concessions and yields a more balanced allocation.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — a clarified, conditional offer reduces later misunderstandings and speeds final agreement.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — checking their need and proposing a thoughtful package builds trust and goodwill.

selected_turns.text: Which option you would like to go for?
selected_turns.why_important: This is the first explicit attempt to solicit the opponent's preferences — it opens the information-exchange phase that determines whether the negotiation will be integrative or zero-sum.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The question is very open and puts the entire burden on the opponent to state priorities; it misses a chance to signal your own priorities or to structure the response so you can discover the opponent's intensity (how much they value items). That reduces the chance of finding value-creating trades and leaves room for misunderstanding.
selected_turns.better_direction: Make the info-seeking more structured and reciprocal: briefly share your own top priorities or tradeable items and ask which items they most/least need or how strongly they prefer them (e.g., rate importance or say 'is firewood most important to you?'). Offer a couple of concrete candidate splits to make it easy for them to react.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — structured info and reciprocal disclosure enable integrative trades and avoid latent conflicts.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — clearer preferences let allocations better match each side's values rather than arbitrary splits.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — concrete choices reduce back-and-forth and speed convergence.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — reciprocal disclosure builds trust and signals cooperativeness.

selected_turns.text: Okay great. Then I will choose 1 firewood, 2 food and 1 water
selected_turns.why_important: This is the key deal-shaping turn: you respond with a concrete allocation that yields firewood to the opponent and proposes a compatible split. It effectively determines the division of items.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although cooperative, the move is presented as a fait accompli without a brief rationale or a check for the opponent's acceptance of specific counts. That can hide residual concerns (e.g., intensity over water vs. food) and misses an opportunity to confirm mutual satisfaction or propose minor compensating trades that could improve joint utility.
selected_turns.better_direction: Accompany the allocation with a short rationale or an explicit check (e.g., 'I value food more so I'll take 2 food — does that work for you?') or propose an alternative bundle swap contingency if they care more about water vs food. That both clarifies motives and invites small adjustments if needed.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: small increase — clarifying motivations can reveal further tradeoffs to improve total value.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — making the reasoning explicit helps the opponent see why the split is equitable relative to preferences.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — explicit checks reduce the risk of later reneging or misunderstandings and speed confirmation.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — transparency and asking for assent signals respect and builds trust.

selected_turns.text: Okay. then let me confirm that same deal.
selected_turns.why_important: This confirmation finalizes the negotiated terms and transitions the conversation to closing and execution — clarifying this prevents errors and locks in the outcome.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The confirmation is short and implicit; it doesn't restate the precise counts for each party or who will submit, so minor ambiguities could remain (e.g., whose counts are which). It misses a brief explicit summary that solidifies mutual understanding.
selected_turns.better_direction: Explicitly restate the agreed allocation in one line (each party's counts) and confirm who will submit or when it will be submitted, so both sides have the same mental record.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: neutral — the allocation is already set, but clarity prevents costly mistakes.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: slight increase — explicit restatement reduces perceived ambiguity about who gets what.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — clear summary and role assignment speed completion and reduce follow-up corrections.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — precise confirmation shows consideration and reduces potential friction.

selected_turns.text: I am most interested in the firewood.  How about I take 2 firewood 1 food and 2 water
selected_turns.why_important: This turn is the first concrete allocation proposal from the assigned agent and therefore sets the negotiation's resource-distribution frame. It shifts the interaction from information-seeking (THEM's question) to a fairly self-centered claim, which strongly affects the opponent's response and the overall tone.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Because it follows a prosocial information-seeking move, replying with a demanding self-oriented offer closes off cooperative options and misses a chance to build value or discover tradeable priorities. It's rigid (takes most of top-priority item) without explaining why or offering trade-offs, so it may unnecessarily limit joint gains and slightly harm rapport.
selected_turns.better_direction: Move toward an integrative, information-sharing stance: acknowledge the question, state your top priority but invite the opponent's priorities or offer a trade-for-value (e.g., indicate willingness to trade some firewood for extra food or water). Offer a rationale for why firewood matters and propose alternative splits that leave the opponent compensated on their likely priorities.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — opening to trade/options creates room for Pareto improvements
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — offering trade-offs or asking opponent preferences yields more balanced outcomes

expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — showing willingness to exchange and asking the other's priorities builds trust

selected_turns.text: Sounds like a plan.  Thanks a bunch.
selected_turns.why_important: This turn accepts and signals closure after the opponent provided a complementary allocation. It is the verbal moment where mutual agreement is acknowledged and could be used to lock in clarity and goodwill.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The reply is polite but thin: it doesn't restate the agreed terms or confirm specifics. That is a missed opportunity to prevent misunderstandings, to acknowledge the opponent's concessions, or to reinforce rapport with a brief explanation of why the split works — all of which would make the deal more durable and fair-feeling.
selected_turns.better_direction: Explicitly restate the agreed allocation and briefly acknowledge the opponent's accommodation (e.g., confirm quantities for each item and thank them for working it out). If you value relationship, add a short appreciation or a small conciliatory comment that recognizes their priorities.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: slight increase — clearer confirmations reduce renegotiation risk
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — acknowledging the other's preference and confirming terms signals respect and perceived fairness
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — explicit confirmation avoids errors and speeds completion
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — appreciation and acknowledgement strengthen rapport

selected_turns.text: I'll go ahead and fill it in...Thanks much
selected_turns.why_important: This is the closing/operational turn before final submission — it indicates who will execute the deal and is the last chance to fix mistakes or clarify terms before a binding action.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: As written, it expresses willingness to fill in but does not restate the deal, confirm that both parties have the same understanding, or specify what 'fill it in' means (who submits, expected timing). That leaves room for errors or minor disputes.
selected_turns.better_direction: Use this moment to explicitly summarize the agreed allocation and confirm submission responsibilities and timing (e.g., 'I'll submit 2 firewood / 1 food / 2 water for me and 1 firewood / 2 food / 1 water for you; I'll submit it now — does that look right?').

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: neutral — summary doesn't change the split but prevents accidental loss of value
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: slight increase — explicit confirmation reassures both parties
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — clarifying submission and timing avoids last-minute confusion
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — thoroughness and clarity signal respect and reliability

selected_turns.text: I want to go on hiking too. Since I don't go alone I need food and water.
selected_turns.why_important: This turn responds to the opponent's explicit demand for water and firewood by restating the agent's own needs. It escalates the competitive frame early on and signals a claim to the same scarce resources, shaping the negotiation toward a zero-sum stance.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: While stating needs is legitimate, this reply missed an opportunity to defuse competition or search for integrative trades. It simply asserts competing claims (proself info) immediately after the opponent's request, which reduced chances of discovering mutually beneficial exchanges and made concession-making more likely later.
selected_turns.better_direction: Instead of only asserting needs, the agent should have acknowledged the opponent's plan and then asked questions or proposed tradeoffs (e.g., ask which item is most critical, offer to trade food for a share of water, or suggest splitting items quantitatively). That would open problem-solving and reveal leverage for mutually beneficial trades.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (by enabling integrative trade and revealing tradeable priorities)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (more balanced outcome by negotiating tradeoffs rather than simple claims)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (less adversarial, more cooperative exploration reduces friction)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (shows willingness to collaborate and understand the other party)

selected_turns.text: This offer is good for me but I need some water too. Can we share the water and have all the firewood? 🙂
selected_turns.why_important: This turn is the key moment where the agent shifts the interaction from competitive to cooperative by proposing a compromise. It attempts to trade water-sharing for conceding firewood, which could open integrative possibilities and de-escalate tension.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The proposal is somewhat vague and lopsided (it concedes all firewood while only asking to 'share' water) and lacks concrete quantities or rationale tied to both parties' priorities. That made it easy for the opponent to reject and return to a self-oriented offer. The move could have been more persuasive and actionable.
selected_turns.better_direction: Make the prosocial move more specific and interest-based: quantify the split (how many water units each), explain why the trade helps both (link to their stated hiking needs), and offer a clear reciprocal concession (e.g., I'll take X food + Y water if you take Z firewood). Framing it as a targeted trade that respects both priorities would increase acceptability.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (more concrete integrative proposal uncovers efficient allocations)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (clear quid pro quo leads to more balanced division)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (specifics reduce ambiguity and make acceptance easier)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (shows constructive bargaining and respect for mutual needs)

selected_turns.text: So I plan to go hiking a lot this trip and I would appreciate a majority of the water and firewood if possible.
selected_turns.why_important: This is the first substantive demand from you and it frames the negotiation as competitive (claiming a majority of two contested goods). Early claims strongly anchor expectations and set a zero-sum tone that shapes subsequent offers and counter-offers.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: As phrased it is a blunt, unilateral demand with no attempt to learn the other side's priorities or open trade opportunities. That increases conflict risk and misses a chance to craft trades that could raise joint value.
selected_turns.better_direction: Soften the opening claim and make it a collaborative inquiry: state your needs but immediately invite their priorities and propose tradeable options (e.g., "I need more water for hiking — what do you most need? Could we consider trading food for water/firewood or sharing water?"). Use conditional language and offer multiple package options rather than demanding a majority outright.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — opening as a trade inquiry creates room for integrative exchanges
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — a less demanding tone encourages mutually acceptable splits
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — reduces early defensiveness and speeds convergence
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — signals respect and willingness to cooperate

selected_turns.text: No, I offer you 3 food, and 1 water.  I would like 2 water and 3 firewood.
selected_turns.why_important: This is a firm rejection of the opponent's request and a hard, self-favoring offer that could lock the discussion into competitive back-and-forth. How you respond here strongly influences whether the dialogue escalates or moves toward compromise.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The response is blunt ('No') and asserts a favorable allocation without acknowledging the opponent's needs or offering clear justifications or multiple options. That rigidity risks breakdown if the opponent resists and misses opportunities to create acceptable package deals.
selected_turns.better_direction: Soften the rejection and pivot to problem-solving: acknowledge their request, give a brief rationale for your priorities, and present 2–3 alternative packages (e.g., share water 2-1 and split firewood 2-1; or I'll take extra water if you take extra food) or ask which items they value most. Use conditional trade language ('If you need all the firewood, could we split water differently?').

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — multiple package offers enable Pareto-improving trades
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — options make tradeoffs clearer and more balanced
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — reduces friction and gives choices for quick convergence
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — shows respect for the other side and reduces hostility

selected_turns.text: I would be willing to sacrifice the majority of food in exchange for water and firewood.
selected_turns.why_important: This is your first explicit concession/trade proposal (offer to give up food for water and firewood). It signals willingness to trade low-value items for high-value ones and therefore is central to the possible agreement structure.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: As stated it prioritizes your own gains without specifying concrete splits, without checking the opponent's priorities, and without packaging alternatives. It can come across as self-serving rather than collaborative, which may provoke counter-demands.
selected_turns.better_direction: Make the concession concrete and collaborative: quantify the trade (propose specific counts), explain the reason briefly, and couple it with a question or alternatives (e.g., 'I can give you X food in exchange for Y water and Z firewood, or alternatively we could split water 2-1 and share firewood'). Invite their input to find a package that meets both needs.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — clearer, quantified offers allow mutually beneficial splits
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — concrete trades make tradeoffs transparent
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — reduces ambiguity and speeds decision-making
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — collaborative wording reduces perceived selfishness

selected_turns.text: I need a minimum of 2 firewood. You can have 2 water. I would now like 2 food.
selected_turns.why_important: This is the first strong, concrete escalation after the opponent rejected your initial demand. It locks the interaction into a competitive, zero-sum bargaining frame by asserting a firm minimum and adding additional claims (2 food) rather than opening trades or exploring underlying needs.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It is rigid and self-focused: it repeats a minimum demand and carves up resources without acknowledging the opponent's stated need for firewood or seeking tradeable preferences. That narrows options and increases likelihood of stalemate or further hard bargaining.
selected_turns.better_direction: Shift away from anchoring/insisting and toward joint problem-solving: acknowledge the opponent's firewood need, ask clarifying questions about why 2 firewood is required, and propose contingent tradeoffs (e.g., 'If you need 2 firewood, would you accept X water now and Y food later?' or offer to split firewood and compensate with more of lower-priority items). Frame proposals as options rather than ultimatums.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: likely increase — opening trade options and asking questions can reveal opportunities for Pareto improvements
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: improve — clearer, dialogic bargaining tends to produce more balanced outcomes than hard demands
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — reducing ultimatums and offering options lowers resistance and speeds convergence
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — acknowledging the other's need and using questions builds trust vs. hard demands

selected_turns.text: I insist on 2 firewood 🙂. I would be happy to let you have 2 water. Are you sure you need 2 food?
selected_turns.why_important: This turn doubles down after the opponent's conditional counter-offer. By reasserting 'I insist' and simultaneously offering only a narrow concession (water) while questioning their food need, you reinforce a tit-for-tat, competitive exchange rather than moving toward a mutually acceptable package.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The mix of insistence and a challenge ('Are you sure you need 2 food?') risks sounding confrontational and may cause the opponent to entrench. It misses an opportunity to propose concrete, integrative packages (e.g., partial splits, conditional trades) or to build empathy that could unlock a smoother agreement.
selected_turns.better_direction: Soften the insistence and convert the question into a collaborative probe or a package offer: acknowledge their constraint, propose multiple options (e.g., 'I can take 2 firewood if we split water/food this way' or 'Would you accept 1 firewood now and 1 if we find more later?'). Offer contingencies or sequencing to reduce perceived loss.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — proposing packages and contingencies creates more mutually beneficial options
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: improve — tradeoffs negotiated openly tend to be seen as fairer than challenged claims
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — offering concrete options lowers friction and speeds convergence
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — reducing insistence and being collaborative will reduce tension and build rapport

selected_turns.text: I would be ok with this situation.

selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The acceptance is vague and minimally communicative. Saying only 'I would be ok with this situation' misses chances to lock in specifics, confirm mutual understanding, express appreciation (which builds rapport), or request a small reciprocal gesture to ensure perceived fairness.
selected_turns.better_direction: Be explicit and constructive: restate the exact allocation you understand (to avoid ambiguity), thank the opponent for flexibility, and, if appropriate, request a short confirmation of the items/timing. Optionally propose one small fairness signal (e.g., 'I appreciate that — can we confirm you'll get X and I'll get Y?') to cement trust.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: improve — explicit confirmation helps both parties feel the deal is equitable
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — clarifying and thanking smooths closing and reduces mistakes
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — appreciation and explicit confirmation strengthen rapport and trust

selected_turns.text: I understand where you're coming from but I'm afraid that is not acceptable.  My family's comfort is my top priority and I will be needing at least 2 firewoods to make sure they are kept warm.  I would also request some extra water.

selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The turn is rigid and framed as a firm rejection rather than probing for underlying priorities or tradeoffs. selected_turns.text: It seems we both have a big need for firewood. 😮 If you insist on 2 firewood, then I insist on getting 2 water AND 2 food along with 1 firewood.

selected_turns.text: Yes. If I'm letting you have more firewood (of which I would like to have more) then I'm going to need more of the other supplies. 🙂

selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The statement is vague and reads like a threat ('I'm going to need more') without specifying concrete trade terms or asking clarifying questions. That ambiguity makes it harder to finalize a fair package and risks unnecessary escalation or delay.

selected_turns.text: awe, that's true,  Now I fell bad for the bears.  I'll take 2 waters and 1 food, you can have all the firewood.

It locks in a zero-sum distribution of the scarce resource (water) and thus determines the outcome and relationship consequences.

selected_turns.text: Water for sure, need to stay extra hydrated.  How about you?
selected_turns.why_important: This is the agent's first clear revelation of preference (water is important). That information shapes all subsequent offers and expectations and enables tradeoffs — it's a critical input to an integrative split.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The turn states the preference but doesn't quantify strength or signal flexibility/willingness to trade. This invites integrative offers and reduces zero-sum framing.

selected_turns.text: Well we don't want to make all the bears sick though haha. Okay so let's try this... one of us gets 2 waters with 1 firewood and 1 food. I think that's as close as we'll get to equal. You can choose.

That allowed the opponent to take the clearly higher-value bundle (2 waters + food) while leaving you mostly firewood — a suboptimal capture of integrative potential.

selected_turns.text: No I want need at least two packages of water.  I can give you two packages of food and all my firewood.

selected_turns.text: It seems we both need the water so If you want 2 water, I would need all the food and firewood as well.

It shapes the distribution of all remaining items and strongly frames the negotiation as zero-sum, influencing whether the parties will move toward agreement or further positional bargaining.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It is overly rigid and punitive: conditioning the opponent getting 2 waters on handing over all food and firewood closes off integrative options (e.g., splitting other goods, side payments, trades over quantities). selected_turns.better_direction: Soften the demand and move from an all-or-nothing stance to a problem-solving offer: acknowledge their need, propose specific, limited tradeoffs (e.g., what combination of food/firewood they'd accept for 2 waters), or propose alternative splits that leave both with enough resources. Ask a clarifying question to uncover flexibility (e.g., which items are least valuable to them) instead of insisting on everything.

selected_turns.text: not bad, but do you mind giving me two wood then?
selected_turns.why_important: This is a direct revision request that pushes for more resources for you after the opponent already presented a conciliatory counter-offer. It shaped the final split by extracting a concession (they agreed), so it materially affected the deal and perceptions of fairness.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Asking for two wood is inconsistent with your earlier stated priority ordering (you said firewood was lowest priority). It reads opportunistic — taking advantage of the opponent's surplus without offering something in return — which can harm perceived fairness and rapport even if it yields short-term gain.
selected_turns.better_direction: Either explain briefly why you now need more wood (so the request doesn't look opportunistic), or offer a small reciprocal concession (e.g., accept one less of something you value less) in exchange. Alternatively, phrase it as a question that acknowledges their needs ('Would you be okay giving me two wood if I take X?') to preserve reciprocity.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: Slight increase if paired with a reciprocal trade; otherwise neutral.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: Improve if you offer reciprocity or explanation; otherwise perceived fairness may drop.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: Increase if presented as a reciprocal trade; otherwise quick agreement may still occur but at a relational cost.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: Strengthen if you show reciprocity/justification; otherwise risk being seen as opportunistic and reduce trust.

selected_turns.text: ok. so its two water, one food and two wood for me then?
selected_turns.why_important: This confirmation locks in the allocation and effectively closes the negotiation. It consolidates the gains you secured (including the additional wood) and finalizes the outcome everyone will live with.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: While efficient, the terse confirmation misses an opportunity to acknowledge the opponent's concession and strengthen rapport (e.g., thank them or offer a small concession or future reciprocity). That would increase perceived fairness and relationship quality without changing the material split.
selected_turns.better_direction: Confirm the allocation but add a brief acknowledgment of the opponent's flexibility or offer a minor reciprocal gesture (thanks, or offer to trade differently next time). This preserves the deal while improving fairness perceptions and relationship.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: Neutral materially, but can increase if it preserves goodwill for future cooperation.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: Improve slightly if you acknowledge concessions or offer reciprocity; otherwise remains the same.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: Already high (deal closed), but adding acknowledgment smooths closure.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: Improve noticeably if you express appreciation or reciprocity; otherwise it stays neutral.

selected_turns.text: Tell me, how stocked up are you on food and firewood?
selected_turns.why_important: This was the first explicit request to surface inventories/preferences after the opponent asked how to share resources. It shaped information flow early and set the agenda for subsequent proposals.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It narrowly asked about food and firewood but omitted water (the opponent had already emphasized water). By not proposing a concrete, integrative split or explicitly addressing water, it left room for the opponent to define the terms with a self-favoring offer later.
selected_turns.better_direction: Broaden the information request to include water and/or immediately propose a tentative, balanced split or principles (e.g., trade firewood for water/food) to anchor a fair, integrative solution—ask conditional questions that tie inventory to trade possibilities rather than only cataloguing items.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (more chance to craft integrative trades)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (anchors a more balanced starting point)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (reduces likelihood of later competitive proposals)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase (signals proactive cooperation and concern for mutual needs)

selected_turns.text: I really don't need that much firewood. How about You get two water, one food and one firewood. And I'll take two food, one water and two firewood.
selected_turns.why_important: This counteroffer was the key turning point that shifted the exchange into explicit competitive bargaining (both sides making claims). It determined the framing of the split and triggered the opponent's follow-up demand for more wood.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It framed the response as a zero-sum claim favoring the speaker's top item (food) rather than leveraging the clear complementarity (both had firewood; both valued water/food) to propose an integrative trade. The absolute re-slicing invited pushback instead of collaborative problem-solving.
selected_turns.better_direction: Pivot from a pure counterclaim to a conditional, integrative proposal: propose a trade package that explains why it's fair (e.g., "I can give X firewood if you give Y water/food") or offer multiple options at different splits so the opponent can pick, which preserves cooperation while protecting your priorities.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (integrative framing can create more value)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (explicit trade rationale reduces perceived theft)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (reduces cycles of demands and counter-demands)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase (less adversarial tone, more mutual accommodation)

selected_turns.text: Sure, you can have two of the firewoods. 🙂
selected_turns.why_important: This concession resolved the immediate dispute (opponent asked for two woods) and moved the parties towards a final split. It materially changed allocations and de-escalated the exchange.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The concession was unconditional and narrowly framed. That missed a chance to secure a reciprocal concession (e.g., clarify that the concession is in exchange for the opponent keeping their water/food allocation) or to state the rationale, which would have protected fairness perceptions and could have extracted a small additional benefit.
selected_turns.better_direction: Make concessions conditional or explain the trade rationale: concede but tie it to a concrete reciprocation ("I'm willing to give two wood if we keep the water/food split we discussed") or explicitly acknowledge the opponent's priority while summarizing the mutual benefits to reinforce fairness.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: small increase (keeps deal stable without reopening demands)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (conditional framing and rationale improve perceived balance)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (clarifies terms and closes lingering uncertainty)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase (shows generosity but also preserves dignity and reciprocity)

selected_turns.text: Hi there.  Hoping it'll be good, and yours as well.  I was hoping we could negotiate so I could get this extra water - I was planning on this being a hiking trip and it's pretty warm and humid out, so I imagine I'll need it.
selected_turns.why_important: This is the opening substantive move by YOU and it sets the negotiation frame: it asserts a personal claim on a scarce good (water). Opening with a direct self-focused request pushed the interaction toward distributive bargaining rather than eliciting the other's priorities or searching for integrative trades.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: As phrased it signals a proself stance and asks for concession without first eliciting the other's needs or proposing trade options. That risks locking in a zero-sum mindset and missing chances to create mutual value or build rapport early.
selected_turns.better_direction: Soften the claim into a collaborative inquiry: briefly state need but immediately invite the other's priorities and offer potential trade options. For example, after stating you need extra water, ask what they care most about and propose a few tradeable, concrete swaps (e.g., "If you need firewood, I can take extra food in exchange"). Emphasize willingness to trade rather than only to obtain.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — opening collaboratively would make integrative options more likely to be explored.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — soliciting the other's needs early supports a more balanced outcome.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — reduces friction by inviting joint problem solving rather than a demand.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — less confrontational language and explicit willingness to trade builds trust.

selected_turns.text: That's a little tough for me but if we can come to an agreement where I get 2 of the food, I'd be willing to play ball.  We both deserve a good camping trip, after all.
selected_turns.why_important: This is the first counter-offer from YOU and it shifts the exchange from THEM's firm demand to a tentative compromise. It creates the opportunity for a reciprocal trade and moves the negotiation toward an integrative outcome.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The phrasing is somewhat vague and reactive ('that's a little tough for me') and the proposed concession (asking for 2 food) isn't tied to a clear reciprocal concession or cost to YOU. It's a missed chance to present a clearer, balanced package that explains what you will do/provide in return and why that makes the deal fair.
selected_turns.better_direction: Frame a concrete, balanced trade: specify what you will give up or do (e.g., take responsibility for gathering X amount of firewood or shorten hike by Y hours) in exchange for the 2 food. Tie the request to a clear cost you’re incurring so the opponent sees the fairness and feasibility. Also ask a confirming question to check the opponent's constraints.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — a clearer reciprocal package can reveal mutually acceptable terms and avoid renegotiation.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — making your concession explicit makes the trade appear more balanced.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — concrete reciprocal terms reduce ambiguity and speed acceptance.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — demonstrating willingness to incur a concrete cost signals cooperativeness and respect.

selected_turns.text: Because I planned to go hiking and it wouldn't leave me time to chop much of any myself.  Even if I did, I'd have to carry it back and that's obviously not very smart in this heat.  But if I had the extra food, I'd be willing to inconvenience myself with getting firewood and cutting my hike a little short because I know the firewood is important to you.
selected_turns.why_important: This turn provides the rationale for YOUR request and, crucially, offers a concession (taking on the cost of getting firewood and shortening your hike). It concretely enables the integrative trade that ultimately leads to agreement — it's a key deal-shaping move.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although cooperative, the move is long-winded and framed as justification rather than a clear quid-pro-quo commitment (no timeline, scope, or explicit confirmation of what you'll deliver). That leaves room for doubt about follow-through and misses an opportunity to lock in terms and reassure the opponent.
selected_turns.better_direction: Keep the explanation brief and convert it into a firm, specific proposal: state exactly what you'll do (e.g., 'I'll gather X pieces of firewood and be back by Y'), request the exact items you need, and ask the opponent if that arrangement is acceptable. Offer a simple contingency (e.g., if I can't find enough wood, we revisit).

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: slight increase — clarifying the concession can solidify the trade and reduce the chance of later renegotiation.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — specifying the cost you bear makes the exchange feel more equitable.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — a concrete, time-bound commitment makes acceptance straightforward.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — clear commitments and contingencies build trust and reduce uncertainty.

selected_turns.text: I don't have any problems letting you have all 3 waters. Would you be willing to give me all 3 firewood? I need extra to keep me warm as I get cold easily.
selected_turns.why_important: This turn is the first concrete allocation proposal from you and it shifts the negotiation into a firm, zero-sum framing (I give you waters only if you give me all firewood). It sets an early bargaining anchor and determines whether the interaction will stay competitive or open up to integrative tradeoffs.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The move is rigid and all-or-nothing: asking for all 3 firewood in return for all 3 waters can feel like a sharp demand and may alienate the other party or miss creative options. It treats resources as strictly distributive instead of exploring the opponent's offered willingness to do work (they later offered to fetch firewood) or other splits of food that could increase joint value. It also neglects validating the opponent's initial water need before demanding the top resource in return.
selected_turns.better_direction: Soften the demand and open space for integrative trade: express understanding of their water need, state your firewood need briefly, and invite alternatives (e.g., propose splitting firewood or linking firewood to a task concession they mentioned). Ask whether they'd be willing to trade portions (e.g., 2 firewood + some other concession) or to do the work to fetch firewood rather than requiring you give up other items. Make it a cooperative proposal that solicits options rather than an ultimatum.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — by opening alternatives and leveraging the opponent's willingness to fetch firewood, more value-creating trades become possible.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — a less all-or-nothing posture allows a more balanced split and perceived reciprocity.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — a softer, exploratory proposal reduces resistance and makes reaching agreement smoother.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — showing flexibility and empathy builds trust and rapport.

selected_turns.text: That's true. We both deserve a good trip. Why do you need more food?
selected_turns.why_important: This turn shifts the interaction away from pure positional bargaining toward information-sharing and opens the door to integrative solutions. Asking for motives is a key move for creating tradeoffs based on different needs rather than fixed allocations.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: While the question is useful, it's brief and neutral; it misses an opportunity to build empathy and to probe specifics that would enable concrete tradeoffs (e.g., how much extra food, how much effort they'd expend fetching firewood, timing constraints). It also could better acknowledge their prior request for water before probing food, which would make the question feel more validating.
selected_turns.better_direction: Make the information request more structured and validating: acknowledge their water need, express that you want to understand constraints (how many extra meals, how hard it would be to fetch firewood, whether they'd be willing to do that), and invite concrete trade options. That both gathers useful data and signals collaborative intent.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: moderate increase — more precise information enables better matching of tasks and goods (e.g., labor-for-food trades).
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — understanding effort and costs allows compensation to be calibrated fairly.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — clearer information reduces misunderstandings and speeds agreement.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — validation and curiosity build trust and reduce perceived aggression.

selected_turns.text: You're right. I do not plan on hiking so you need more food than me. If I am correct, would I get 3 firewood/1 food while you get 3 water/2 food?
selected_turns.why_important: This is the concrete deal-shaping move that operationalizes the earlier information exchange: it translates needs into a specific allocation and effectively resolves the main conflicts. It determines the final division of high-priority items for both parties.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although cooperative, the proposal omits implementation details and doesn't explicitly link the allocation to the concession the other party offered (fetching firewood). That leaves potential ambiguity (who actually does the work, timing, or whether smaller splits of firewood/food might be fairer). Small clarifications could reduce the risk of post-agreement friction and make the trade feel more balanced.
selected_turns.better_direction: When proposing the split, briefly tie it to the concession the opponent offered (e.g., confirm that they'll fetch or gather firewood or accept the work trade), or add a simple implementation clause (who will fetch what, or whether firewood can be shared if needed). Alternatively, offer a small adjustment if it increases perceived fairness (e.g., clarify food portions or timing). These additions keep the deal cooperative but reduce ambiguity.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: slight increase — clarifying implementation prevents wasted effort and ensures the deal actually delivers value.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — explicit linkage to the opponent's concession makes the trade feel more equitable.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — minor clarifications reduce the chance of later disputes and make acceptance smoother.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — showing attention to follow-through and the other's effort builds goodwill.

selected_turns.text: Can I get 2 firewood?
selected_turns.why_important: This turn represents a clear shift from the cooperative tone the other party set (they accepted the earlier allocation and asked about firewood) to an explicit self-oriented claim. That shift could have been a turning point toward competitive bargaining, risking trust and perceived fairness.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although the opponent accepted the earlier split and had said they could give up firewood or water, asking bluntly for 2 of 3 firewood is a claim-oriented move that is unnecessarily zero-sum. It ignores an opportunity to preserve the integrative framing by either offering a rationale, proposing a fair split, or seeking the opponent's preference for firewood allocation. It's therefore suboptimal for long-term agreement quality and relationship.
selected_turns.better_direction: Stay consistent with the prosocial frame: either propose a fair/explicit full-package allocation that explains the rationale (e.g., tie firewood to a concession you gave on another item), ask the opponent how they'd prefer to split firewood, or suggest a 1–1 split and offer a small trade if you truly need 2. In short, make this a collaborative question or a justified trade rather than a raw claim.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: likely increase (by keeping conversation integrative and enabling mutually beneficial tradeoffs)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (by avoiding the appearance of grabbing the scarce item and by offering a transparent trade or split)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (reduced chance of friction and smoother close)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (preserves trust and reciprocity)

selected_turns.text: I think we can start off with 3 water for me and 3 food for you
selected_turns.why_important: This is the first concrete allocation proposal and it directly addresses both parties' stated top priorities — a strong integrative move that could have locked in a high-quality, fair deal.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The offer is good but incomplete: it doesn't resolve firewood and therefore leaves room for later self-oriented claims. Also it could have been framed as a full-package proposal (including firewood) or explicitly invited the opponent's input on the remaining item to preserve the cooperative momentum.
selected_turns.better_direction: Present a complete package (including firewood) or explicitly ask the opponent how they prefer to split the remaining item as part of the proposal. Alternatively, present this offer while signaling flexibility on firewood and proposing a simple equitable rule (e.g., split firewood 1–1 unless you need more) to avoid later zero-sum moves.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (by turning the offer into a complete, integrative package that reduces negotiation friction)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (clear allocation across all items avoids perceived last-minute grabbing)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (fewer open issues, smoother close)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (shows consideration and reduces surprise demands)

selected_turns.text: It'd be nice if we can both get an even split right?
selected_turns.why_important: This early normative move sets fairness as the negotiation frame. Because the opponent had just shared their priority for food, this moment was an opportunity to convert that information into a tailored integrative proposal.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Saying 'even split' is vague and may come across as ignoring the asymmetry the opponent revealed (they need more food). It misses a chance to acknowledge their need and propose a concrete trade that respects their high priority while still achieving balance overall.
selected_turns.better_direction: A stronger move would acknowledge the opponent's food priority and then suggest a specific, principled split that reflects that (e.g., 'I hear food is critical — how about I take more water and you take more food, and we split firewood?'). Framing fairness in terms of compensating differences (not strict equality of items) would be more integrative.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (by enabling tradeoffs based on revealed preferences)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (allocations more aligned with each party's needs rather than blind equality)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (less need for later concessions once needs are addressed)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (signals understanding and responsiveness)

selected_turns.text: Sure
selected_turns.why_important: This single-word acceptance conceded the opponent's concrete request for 2 firewood and effectively locked in an uneven allocation (they get 3 water + 2 firewood). It directly advanced the deal toward the opponent-favorable outcome and removed room for further bargaining over a scarce item.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It's overly passive and undifferentiated — by accepting immediately the agent missed the chance to negotiate a fairer split of firewood, to trade on items of differing value, or to ask for a small concession elsewhere. Because the opponent had already taken maximal claims on water, accepting their additional claim without a counterfactual reduces fairness and possibly joint value.
selected_turns.better_direction: Resist immediate acceptance and instead propose a concrete, simple alternative (e.g., an even split of firewood or a small trade), or ask a brief clarifying/probing question about their needs before conceding. Even a short counteroffer or request for rationale would preserve bargaining space.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (by enabling a trade/counteroffer that could reallocate items more efficiently)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (prevents one side from capturing disproportionate share of multiple high-priority items)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: slight decrease short-term (adds one more round) but increase long-term (more balanced, less regret)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase (shows you're willing to negotiate rather than simply acquiescing, which can build mutual respect)

selected_turns.text: That sounds good. What about firewood?
selected_turns.why_important: This turn accepted the opponent's proposed division of food and water without pushing back and immediately moved to the remaining item. That acceptance framed the negotiation as settled on the major items, giving the opponent leverage on the last scarce resource (firewood).
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Saying 'That sounds good' too quickly was a missed opportunity to test the proposal, clarify tradeoffs, or propose an alternative distribution that might be more balanced. Because several items remain in play, explicitly acknowledging agreement while proposing tradeoffs or asking about their priorities would have opened integrative options.
selected_turns.better_direction: Instead of blanket acceptance, briefly acknowledge positives and then propose a concrete division rule for firewood (e.g., 1/1 split) or request the opponent's priority for firewood and suggest a trade (e.g., I keep some extra food if you take more firewood). This keeps bargaining space and signals willingness to find a fair split.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (by creating room for mutually beneficial trades)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (reduces chance opponent sweeps multiple high-value items)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: neutral to increase (may require one more exchange but leads to more stable agreement)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase (shows cooperative but engaged behavior, not passive concession)

selected_turns.text: Well some needs are higher than others.  I need more food but I can give up some firewood or water.
selected_turns.why_important: This disclosure of priorities and flexibility is the key integrative signal that could enable tradeoffs (you value food most and are flexible on the other items). It shaped what offers were feasible and how the opponent allocated items.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It's useful information but vague — it doesn't quantify how much food is needed, what the minimum acceptable share is, or exactly how much water/firewood you're willing to give up. That vagueness made it easy for the opponent to propose an allocation that satisfied their preferences while still taking advantage of ambiguity.
selected_turns.better_direction: Be more specific: state approximate quantities or thresholds (e.g., 'I need at least X units of food' or 'I can give up up to Y water or Z firewood') and invite the opponent to share priorities. Framing these as tradeable offers (e.g., 'I need 2 food but can give you 1 more firewood in exchange') would more actively steer towards efficient splits.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (clearer constraints enable better Pareto trades)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (reduces exploitation of ambiguity)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (quicker convergence on feasible deals)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase (transparency fosters trust and smoother bargaining)

selected_turns.text: In this extra package I need two food
selected_turns.why_important: This is the first explicit allocation request from you; it sets a competitive, self-centered tone and frames the negotiation as zero-sum. Early moves shape expectations and make later integrative tradeoffs harder.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: As stated it is a bare demand with no exploration of the opponent's priorities or willingness to trade. That makes it easy for the opponent to resist and for the interaction to escalate into reciprocal hard bargaining. There's clear room to make the opening more collaborative or diagnostic.
selected_turns.better_direction: Instead of only demanding two food, signal willingness to trade or ask about the opponent's priorities (open with a conditional request or a question). Shift from a hard claim to a trade-seeking frame (e.g., explain need briefly but invite what they value most and offer what you can give in return).

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (opens opportunity for mutually beneficial trades)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (more transparent exchange of priorities, less anchoring on one party)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (less immediate resistance and fewer rejections)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (less confrontational, more cooperative tone)

selected_turns.text: please consider my request i'm big in size so please accept to give two pack of food and 2 water and 1 firewood
selected_turns.why_important: This follows a demanding counter-offer from the opponent and is a consequential reply: it reiterates your own allocation without addressing the opponent's stated demands. It anchors the interaction into a distributive exchange and misses a direct response to their priorities.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It's still a self-favoring request supported by an emotional plea rather than a strategic counter that trades on differences in value. Given the opponent showed they value water/firewood highly, this was a missed opportunity to propose a package that trades items they care about for the food you want.
selected_turns.better_direction: Respond to the opponent's offer by either (a) asking clarifying questions about which items they value most, or (b) proposing a conditional trade that concedes on items the opponent values (e.g., offer more water or firewood in exchange for two food) — i.e., convert the exchange into an explicit trade-off rather than repeating the demand.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (creates room to exploit differing valuations)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (more explicit give-and-take instead of unilateral claims)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (more likely to find a package acceptable to both)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (shows responsiveness to the opponent's stated position)

selected_turns.text: Submit-Deal
selected_turns.why_important: You moved to finalize a deal immediately after the opponent restated/clarified the allocation. That procedural jump to submit a deal locked in your terms prematurely and directly produced a rejection rather than prompting confirmation or small concessions.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Submitting the deal before explicitly confirming mutual understanding or addressing any implicit concerns was mistimed. The opponent's prior turn was prosocial/clarifying, so the better move would have been to acknowledge or confirm the restatement rather than immediately formalizing a deal.
selected_turns.better_direction: Pause to confirm the opponent's paraphrase and ask whether they agree or have concerns; if there is hesitation, offer a small, targeted concession or an explicit conditional acceptance to bridge the gap before submitting. In short, convert the submit into a confirmation/mini-concession step.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: small increase (reduces wasted back-and-forth and avoids collapse)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (shows willingness to ensure mutual understanding and adjust)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (less likely to trigger rejection, smoother closing)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (more respectful and cooperative closing behavior)

selected_turns.text: i will give you that in exchange for 1 food and 3 water with 2 firewood
selected_turns.why_important: This is the agent's first concrete counter-offer and it substantially shapes the distributional frame of the negotiation — it concedes the opponent's demand for extra food but extracts almost all remaining resources, locking the bargaining into a zero-sum stance.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The offer is highly greedy and unilateral, misses opportunities to trade on different item values, gives no rationale or options, and escalates competition rather than opening integrative possibilities. It risks rejection and harms the chance of a high-quality joint agreement.
selected_turns.better_direction: Soften the demand and shift toward value-creating trades: propose a smaller claim on water/firewood, ask which items the opponent values less, offer multiple package options (e.g., take the extra food plus one water, or accept two water if you get one extra food), and provide a brief justification to make the trade seem fairer.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — opening trade options and reducing greed enables more mutually valuable splits.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — a less lopsided offer will be perceived as fairer.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — a softer, option-rich proposal reduces likelihood of outright rejection and speeds agreement.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — appearing willing to compromise and explain reduces hostility and builds trust.

selected_turns.text: u said i will have 2 food 2 firewood and 1 water?
selected_turns.why_important: This clarification both asserts a favorable allocation for the agent and frames the agent as ready to accept that split. It influences whether the opponent moves to finalize or re-negotiate and can prematurely anchor the outcome in the agent's favor.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Framing the question purely as confirmation of a beneficial split is self-oriented and misses a chance to explore why the opponent insisted on that deal, what tradeoffs they might accept, or to propose small concessions to reach agreement. It can lock the conversation into positional bargaining.
selected_turns.better_direction: Instead of simply restating a favorable split, ask an open clarification that surfaces the opponent's priorities or constraints (e.g., ask whether food is their top priority or whether they'd accept an alternative mix), or immediately follow confirmation with a tentative, fair counter-proposal offering choices.

selected_turns.why_important: This turn is a clear shift from the cooperative tone: the opponent asked about water and you responded with a direct self-interested demand. That pivot can reintroduce zero-sum pressure and threatens to derail cooperation just as the partner engaged constructively.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It's framed as a unilateral demand rather than a joint problem-solving move. Given the partner signaled interest in water, a blunt request for two waters increases contention and misses an opportunity to trade in a way that preserves joint value.
selected_turns.better_direction: Instead of issuing a demand, explain the need briefly, ask how important water is to them, and propose a concrete trade/compromise (e.g., offer a concession on firewood or food in exchange for one water, or suggest splitting water) so you stay in an integrative frame.

selected_turns.text: Oh, I want you too as well😮
selected_turns.why_important: This turn reinforces rapport and shared concern right after the opponent validated mutual survival. It's a relational moment that could be converted into an actionable offer to close or improve the deal.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It's empathetic but passive — it doesn't translate goodwill into a concrete next-step (e.g., a clear proposal or an explicit concession). That wastes a chance to convert alignment into a faster, fairer agreement.
selected_turns.better_direction: Leverage the empathy by proposing a specific, balanced next step (for example, suggest how water could be split or propose a package that addresses both parties' survival needs) or ask a clarifying question that moves toward a resolution.

selected_turns.text: Wow. I’m alone here. I could use an extra food and water.
selected_turns.why_important: This turn marks a clear escalation of a zero-sum framing: after the opponent justified wanting extra wood, you immediately counter with your own direct claim. That increases competition over scarce items and sets a more adversarial tone early in bargaining.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It's a straightforward self-centered request with no concession, empathy, or attempt to explore tradeoffs. That both hardens positions and misses an early chance to build trust or find integrative trades (e.g., sequencing, contingent offers, or asking for what they'd accept).
selected_turns.better_direction: A better move would acknowledge the opponent's need, gather more information or propose a conditional trade rather than an unconditional claim — e.g., express understanding of their situation, ask what they absolutely need, or suggest a package you could accept in exchange for food and water. This keeps the door open to cooperative problem-solving.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — moving from blunt claims toward conditional trading can create mutually beneficial splits
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — acknowledging the other's need and trading explicitly tends to produce fairer-seeming allocations
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — reducing escalation and opening trade options makes agreement more likely and faster
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — empathy and conditional offers reduce tension and build trust

selected_turns.text: I understand. I would like one food and one water. How much wood do you want for that?
selected_turns.why_important: This is the first concrete bargaining proposal from you — it frames the exchange and shapes the deal space. It moves the negotiation from information to concrete trade, so it strongly influences the eventual allocation.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although it proposes a trade, it is posed in a terse, transactional way (demanding specific items) without fully recognizing the opponent's larger group constraint or offering alternatives. That can feel rigid and maintain a zero-sum frame rather than opening integrative options (e.g., substitutions, split timing, or shared use).
selected_turns.better_direction: Soften and expand the proposal: acknowledge their needs again, present the trade as a suggestion (and invite counter-options), or offer multiple packages (e.g., different splits that preserve their warmth while meeting your food/water needs). This communicates flexibility and invites collaborative problem-solving.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — offering options or showing flexibility can uncover trades that make both better off
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — cooperative framing and multiple options tend to yield outcomes seen as fairer
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — inviting counters and showing flexibility reduces stalemate risk
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — a less transactional tone and visible concern for the other's constraints builds rapport

selected_turns.text: So I’ll give you one wood and you give me one food and one water.
selected_turns.why_important: This turn finalizes the reciprocal allocation that resolves the negotiation; it cements the division of goods and effectively closes the substantive bargaining.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: While cooperative and effective, it's somewhat terse and transactional. Small additions (acknowledgement, a brief confirmation of timing/expectations, or an expression of appreciation) could reduce residual friction and strengthen relationship for future interactions.
selected_turns.better_direction: When finalizing, explicitly confirm that both sides are satisfied with the split and, if appropriate, add a brief note of thanks or a check for logistical details (timing/any contingencies). That preserves goodwill without changing the deal content.

selected_turns.text: I'm doing well. I was hoping I could have the extra firewood because my group decided to camp for a few extra days and want to make sure we have enough
selected_turns.why_important: This is your first substantive move and it sets the tone toward a zero-sum claim (you assert a need for the extra firewood). Early anchoring toward a self-focused justification strongly shapes the remainder of the negotiation and can trigger reciprocal defensiveness from the other side.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although it's reasonable to explain your need, the utterance is entirely self-oriented and persuasive without signaling any openness to trade or mutual gain. That increases the chance of conflict or rigid haggling instead of opening integrative possibilities (e.g., trading wood for food/water). It therefore leaves meaningful room for improvement.
selected_turns.better_direction: Keep the justification but soften the zero-sum framing and immediately signal willingness to explore trades or compensation (e.g., acknowledge the other person's needs, propose potential exchanges or ask about their priorities). Framing your need as a basis for a cooperative trade rather than an absolute claim would be better.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (by opening integrative trade options rather than locking a zero-sum claim)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (by appearing willing to balance needs rather than simply take)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (reduces defensive responses and accelerates cooperative proposals)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (more empathic/cooperative tone builds trust)

selected_turns.text: How many would you like? I just want to make sure we have enough good and water because there are so many of us
selected_turns.why_important: This turn follows the opponent’s disclosure that they could use extra food and water. Instead of acknowledging that and pivoting toward a cooperative trade, you asked a quantity but immediately re-centered your own group's need — reinforcing a competitive frame and missing a chance to build goodwill or propose a concrete exchange.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It is improvable because you respond with more self-justification rather than engaging the opponent's expressed needs or proposing tradeoffs. That perpetuates a tit-for-tat self-oriented exchange rather than shifting to integrative bargaining.
selected_turns.better_direction: Respond first by acknowledging their situation, then ask a clarifying but collaborative question (e.g., confirm what matters most to them) or propose a tentative trade (e.g., 'I can give one wood if you can do one food and one water'). Show willingness to give while requesting compensation, rather than only restating your scarcity.

selected_turns.text: I think we could get by with two pieces of wood and I would be glad to give you the other one
selected_turns.why_important: This is the key deal-shaping concession: you move into cooperative territory and propose a concrete split of wood. It directly enables the opponent to request food and water and makes a concrete trade feasible—it materially impacts the final allocation.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although cooperative, it’s a bit vague about the linkage between wood and the requested food/water (no explicit quid pro quo) and it doesn’t quantify the full trade terms (e.g., make clear you expect one food + one water in exchange). That leaves room for slight misunderstandings or re-opening of negotiation.
selected_turns.better_direction: Make the trade explicit and conditional: tie your concession to the opponent’s offer (e.g., state you’ll take two wood and give one wood if they give you one food and one water). Confirm quantities and timelines so there’s no ambiguity.

selected_turns.text: How about you take all the water and one of the firewoods, and I can have all the food and 2 of the firewoods

Because it came right after the opponent explicitly shared needs, it shaped the rest of the interaction and led quickly to agreement.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The offer is self-serving (proself) and was presented without an explicit rationale or signals of fairness/flexibility. That risks locking the negotiation into a zero-sum frame and misses an opportunity to increase perceived fairness or explore integrative trades that could raise joint value or trust. Even though the opponent accepted, a slightly different approach could have improved fairness and relationship while keeping the agent's priorities.

selected_turns.text: I won't be doing a lot of hiking, but will probably have a few campfires with food

It missed the chance to make priorities explicit (e.g., 'firewood most important, then food, water lowest') or to describe which items you'd be willing to give up, which would facilitate integrative trades and signal fairness.

selected_turns.text: Which supplies do you need?
selected_turns.why_important: This question invited the opponent to reveal preferences and enabled the integrative exchange that followed. Asking for needs is a good cooperative move that sets the stage for tradeoffs.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It's a generic request; a more targeted question (e.g., asking which item is essential vs. negotiable, how many campfires expected, or acceptable trades) would have elicited richer, more actionable information sooner and made subsequent proposals easier to craft fairly.
selected_turns.better_direction: Ask more specific, preference-revealing questions (priority ranking, necessity vs. optional items, or what they'd accept in trade). That encourages concrete information that supports mutually beneficial offers.
