selected_turns.text: Well, I really need water in case we get really thirsty from hiking. I also need firewood in case it gets colder and we need more. It would be cool to have more food added for dessert like s'mores, but it's not really necessary.
selected_turns.why_important: This turn is the agent's clear statement of priorities (water highest, firewood medium, food low). Revealing priorities is crucial because it enables integrative trades and shapes any offers the partner makes.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although it shares priorities, it stops short of giving negotiable boundaries or explicit trade flexibility (e.g., which items the agent would be willing to trade, minimums, or conditional preferences). That leaves the partner guessing about where there's room to trade and misses a chance to steer the allocation toward a mutually better split.
selected_turns.better_direction: Make the preference statement more actionable for trade: signal which items are non-negotiable vs. flexible and indicate possible concessions (without making a hard offer). For example, indicate minimum needs for water and where you could concede (food or some firewood) or suggest what you value enough to trade for something the opponent cares about.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: Likely increase — clearer flexibility would reveal potential mutually beneficial trades and avoid zero-sum proposals.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: Improve — making boundaries explicit reduces chance of one-sided allocations and helps craft a more balanced split.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: Improve — partner can craft targeted offers rather than guessing, speeding agreement.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: Improve — transparent, cooperative signaling builds trust and reduces friction.

selected_turns.text: Let me think about it. What supplies do you need?
selected_turns.why_important: This is the agent's immediate response to a concrete proself offer from the partner. It's a pivot point: the agent can accept, counter, or attempt to open integrative trade. How the agent responds here strongly shapes whether negotiation stays zero-sum or becomes collaborative.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Asking the partner again what they need is cooperative but redundant (the partner had already proposed and later clarified their usage). This misses a tactical opportunity to capitalize on revealed preferences (e.g., opponent values food highly and water less) by making a conditional counteroffer or probing trade-space more narrowly. The current move keeps the initiative with the opponent and delays productive bargaining.
selected_turns.better_direction: Instead of re-asking, use the information just revealed to propose a conditional counteroffer or a focused clarifying question (e.g., ask which trade they'd accept to give up an extra firewood, or explicitly offer to take water in exchange for conceding something the opponent values). Take the initiative to frame integrative options that preserve your top priority (water) while giving them what they value.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: Improve — constructive, informed engagement signals respect for the partner's needs and willingness to find a fair solution.

selected_turns.text: I couldn't live well with myself if I would let you die. You can have the two waters and 2 firewoods. Please be careful with your meds.
selected_turns.why_important: This turn finalizes a generous, emotionally charged concession that directly determines the material split. It closes the deal in a strongly prosocial way, and thus has a major effect on fairness and the agent's own outcome.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although empathic language builds rapport, this turn appears to over-concede (giving two waters and two firewoods) beyond the prior negotiated proposal. The moral phrasing risks encouraging excessive future concessions and reduces the agent's own payoff and fairness of the split. It also slightly blurs the agreed specifics (emotional language instead of confirming the exact allocation).
selected_turns.better_direction: Keep the empathetic tone but avoid over-conceding or moralizing language that gives away more than agreed. Confirm the exact allocation that preserves your priorities (e.g., restate the agreed split) and offer supportive words without changing the material terms. If you want to be generous, make it explicit and conditional (e.g., offer one extra item only if they truly need it).

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: small decrease (current over-concession reduces your payoff without increasing total resources); preventing the extra concession could maintain or slightly raise joint perceived efficiency.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase if adjusted (avoiding the extra concession would make the split fairer to both sides); current turn reduces perceived fairness for you.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: neutral to slightly improved (empathy helps closure), but clarity about terms would make agreement cleaner and reduce misunderstandings.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improved by empathy; maintaining empathy while avoiding over-concession keeps relationship strong without sacrificing fairness.

selected_turns.text: I see. And I would like 2 extra packages of Food and 2 packages of  Water as well.
selected_turns.why_important: This early turn directly competes with the opponent on scarce items (especially water) and sets a zero-sum tone. It shapes the subsequent exchange by creating overlap in top priorities rather than opening integrative possibilities.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Responding to an urgent claim with a competing demand escalates conflict and misses an opportunity to learn about why water is important or to propose mutually beneficial trades. It hardens positions and makes agreement harder than a small, clarifying, or integrative move would have.
selected_turns.better_direction: Shift from a competing demand to an information-seeking or empathic response: acknowledge their request and ask about urgency/constraints, or immediately offer a trade (e.g., offer to take food if they truly need the water) to open integrative possibilities. That preserves your interests while reducing zero-sum framing.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase if changed (opens room for integrative trades instead of head-to-head demands).
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (more balanced trade-offs rather than immediate competition).
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (reduces escalation and increases willingness to exchange).
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (less confrontational, more cooperative communication).

selected_turns.text: Since carrying the extra would could make your dehydration worse, I'll take any firewood you don't want. But I'll give you the 2 waters and firewood then for the 3 food and 1 firewood. Deal?
selected_turns.why_important: This is the key deal-shaping turn that converts expressed needs into a concrete trade. It directly influences what each side will get and thereby the final outcome, so small changes here could materially improve joint value, fairness, and clarity.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The proposal is helpful but a bit muddled and slightly inconsistent (language and the distribution of firewood/water could be clearer). It also misses a chance to explicitly tie the trade to both parties' highest priorities (guaranteeing the opponent enough water while securing your high-value food), or to offer alternatives if the opponent can't carry firewood.
selected_turns.better_direction: Make the offer clearer and more systematic: state the exact split of each item, briefly justify how the split meets both parties' top concerns, and propose one or two alternative splits (e.g., if you can't carry firewood, accept 2 water + 0 firewood in exchange for food) to increase flexibility. Invite confirmation of how many items they can physically carry.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (clarity and alternative options can find better-balanced solutions).
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (explicit connection of trade to needs makes distribution feel more justified).
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (clearer, easier for the opponent to accept or counter).
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (shows thoughtfulness and respect for their physical constraints).

selected_turns.text: No, of course not. You can have the extra of those if you'd like. It is really the water that is the most important to me.
selected_turns.why_important: This turn follows the opponent's prosocial, clarifying question and responds by reverting to a firm, self-oriented allocation. That shift (prosocial -> proself) is a turning point in tone: it risks undermining the cooperative frame the opponent opened and can reduce trust or willingness to explore integrative trades.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although the speaker's medical need is real, the response is primarily a claim+concession framed as an allocation rather than a collaborative problem-solving move. It misses the opportunity to acknowledge the opponent's concern, build rapport, and invite tradeoffs that could create value or share burdens (e.g., carrying or timing). The move is consequential because it can (unnecessarily) harden positions even though mutual benefits are still available.
selected_turns.better_direction: Acknowledge and thank the opponent for their concern, briefly state the medical need, then invite a joint solution (e.g., ask about their priorities and constraints and propose concrete trade options that account for carrying burden). In other words, keep the need claim but pair it with empathy and an offer to find a mutually acceptable split or burden-sharing arrangement.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — more information and collaborative framing would open integrative trade possibilities (e.g., compensations or carrying tradeoffs).
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — acknowledging the other's concerns and proposing tradeoffs is likely to lead to a more balanced, perceived-fair split.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — retaining a cooperative frame and inviting options reduces friction and speeds agreement.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — gratitude and joint problem-solving would boost rapport and trust.

selected_turns.text: Okay, can I have the 2 waters and 2 firewood then? I will give you all of the food, and 1 of the water. Do you need any firewood at all?
selected_turns.why_important: This is a concrete allocation proposal that shapes the final split. It reduces the opponent's water request and locks in a self-favouring division. Because it directly proposes who gets what, it strongly influences whether agreement will be smooth or contested.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The offer is firm and framed in zero-sum terms; it doesn't show that the speaker considered the opponent's stated preferences (they said they want food and water) nor does it explicitly address carrying concerns mentioned earlier. It's therefore somewhat brittle and could have been perceived as bargaining pressure rather than cooperative problem-solving.
selected_turns.better_direction: Soften the proposal by (a) briefly acknowledging the opponent's priorities, (b) explaining why the extra firewood is helpful (or not) for you, and (c) offering a small compensating concession or contingency (e.g., if you take extra firewood, you give them an additional small item or help carry). Ask a confirming question that invites alternative suggestions rather than demanding acceptance.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: moderate increase — adding compensating concessions or contingencies makes the split feel more balanced.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — a collaborative tone and explicit contingencies reduce resistance and speed acceptance.

selected_turns.text: I have a medical condition that requires medication, and this medication requires me to drink an extra amount of water or else I will dehydrate.

This pairs influence with prosocial framing.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — pairing need with joint problem-solving can surface compensating items or contingencies that expand mutual value.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — framing the need together with trade options is likely to yield a division seen as more equitable.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — expressing the need while showing concern for the other's outcomes improves trust and goodwill.

selected_turns.text: I need 3 water, 2 firewood, and one food. It would be good if I got that!  🙂🙂🙂☹️☹️
selected_turns.why_important: This is the first concrete allocation demand and an early anchor. It immediately pushed the interaction toward a zero-sum frame (the opponent promptly said it was impossible), which shaped the whole negotiation trajectory.

selected_turns.text: I get dehydrated easily. I need to water so that doesn't happen. 🙂
selected_turns.why_important: This is the first substantive response to the opponent's probing and it keeps the interaction in a proself/info loop. That reciprocal pattern makes the negotiation more adversarial and reduces chances for creative tradeoffs.

selected_turns.text: Yes, I would be happy with two food and two firewood. That would leave you with one food and one firewood as well as all of the water.
selected_turns.why_important: This is the deal-shaping turn where you translate needs into a firm allocation that the opponent then accepts. It closes the space for further integrative trade and determines the final distribution of utilities between parties.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although it secures your priorities, it's presented as a take-it-or-leave-it offer without presenting alternative packages or acknowledging the opponent's priorities — a missed opportunity to propose creative trades that could improve fairness or joint value (for example, contingent sharing, scheduling use of water, or small compensations).
selected_turns.better_direction: Instead of a single firm split, present 2–3 package options that balance both parties' top needs (e.g., option A: you get 2F+2FW, they get all water; option B: you take 2F+1FW but they get an extra water-sharing arrangement; option C: share firewood but rotate usage). Ask which they prefer and invite a small tweak to improve fairness.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (option-based bargaining can uncover Pareto improvements)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (gives opponent meaningful choices and seen tradeoffs)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (choices lower friction and speed decision-making)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (shows concern for mutual interests and fosters goodwill)

selected_turns.text: All the food is too much, I would need at least one package of the food for my group. I was thinking you could have 2 of the food packages and I have 1, then I get all the water and the majority of the firewood. That leaves you with 2 of the high priority food packages and a package of firewood to cook the food.
selected_turns.why_important: This is the first concrete counteroffer that shaped the division of scarce items (food, firewood, water). It set the main trade-off (food vs. water/firewood) and therefore strongly influenced subsequent reactions and the final outcome.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although cooperative in intent, it ignored a concrete worry the opponent would raise (that one package of firewood may not be enough to cook two food packages). The offer emphasizes the speaker's retention of water and most firewood without explicitly addressing the opponent's cooking need, which made the opponent respond that the split is "quite unfair." The move was consequential but could have better prevented escalation and created more joint value.
selected_turns.better_direction: Before or while proposing the split, explicitly acknowledge the opponent's cooking constraint and either (a) ask how much firewood they need to cook two packages, or (b) offer a compensating adjustment (e.g., an extra firewood package, or a water-for-firewood trade) or propose a contingency (share firewood or swap some water later). Focus the offer on meeting both parties' critical needs rather than only asserting retained items.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — by surfacing the opponent's real constraint, you enable package deals that satisfy both needs rather than locking into a zero-sum split.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — explicitly addressing the cooking need makes the offer feel more balanced and attentive to the other's priorities.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — reduces likelihood of protest/reluctant rejection and speeds convergence by removing a clear objection.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — shows empathy and reduces perceived selfishness, building trust.

selected_turns.text: I understand. What part of the deal is unfair to you? You get the majority of the highest priority item (food) and I get the majority of the lower priority items (water and firewood).
selected_turns.why_important: This turn is the first explicit de-escalation attempt after the opponent complained the proposal was unfair. How you respond here strongly affects whether the dialogue moves toward compromise or entrenches the dispute.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The reply is somewhat defensive and rationalizing (re-stating why the proposal is fair), which can come across as dismissing the opponent's emotion or concrete concern. That invites further complaint instead of cooperation. It missed the chance to elicit specifics and to acknowledge the opponent's constraint before proposing a fix.
selected_turns.better_direction: Use more empathic, open-ended language and invite specifics: validate their feeling first, ask what outcome would make the trip enjoyable for them, and signal willingness to adjust (e.g., offer to consider giving an extra firewood package or trading some water). Avoid repeating the same justification; instead, pivot to problem-solving and offer concrete options to address the cooking concern.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — by eliciting the real objection and offering concrete fixes you open the door to workable tradeoffs.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — validation plus willingness to adjust will make outcomes feel more equitable.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — reduces friction and speeds toward a mutually acceptable compromise.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — empathetic engagement reduces tension and builds rapport.

selected_turns.text: So is your counter that you get 2 packages of food, 0 of water, and 2 of firewood?
selected_turns.why_important: This clarification turn confirmed the opponent's concrete counteroffer and directly shaped the final allocation that was soon accepted. Confirming facts prevented misunderstanding and enabled closure.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: While effective at confirming the offer, it was a missed opportunity to pair that confirmation with a small conditional concession or a quick probe about feasibility (e.g., whether two firewood packages suffice to cook two food packages) or to propose a smoothing trade that would make the deal fairer (e.g., offer one extra small resource or future favor).
selected_turns.better_direction: After confirming the counter, add one concise, constructive follow-up: ask whether one firewood will suffice per food package or suggest a tiny adjustment that addresses their stated concern (e.g., 'If two firewood are necessary, could we swap X?'). This keeps the confirmation but nudges toward solving the cooking constraint rather than passively accepting a painful split.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: small increase — adding a quick verification of feasibility can avoid a failed implementation or dissatisfaction later.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — addressing a practical shortfall improves perceived equity of the deal.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — small, constructive follow-ups reduce the chance of later regret or re-opening of the dispute.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — showing care for the other's practical needs strengthens trust.

Use concrete quantities but leave room for exchange rather than staking an uncompromising claim.

selected_turns.text: Hmm, ok, thats pretty fair. I do have a larger group going as well, so would it be okay if I got 2 extra food then?
selected_turns.why_important: This turn is a deal-shaping moment: THEM proposed a concrete trade (2 waters for all 3 firewood) and YOU respond by accepting the trade frame but immediately ask for extra food. That request determined the final allocation and could have shifted the interaction toward a more zero-sum dynamic or provoked resistance if handled poorly.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although it worked here, the move is a same-stance (proself→proself) escalation: you accepted a competitive offer and then pushed for more for yourself rather than opening integrative options or explicitly trading something in return. It missed an opportunity to ask about the opponent's constraints or to propose a package that preserves their key priorities while compensating you—so it risks appearing grabby and could reduce perceived fairness or future cooperation in a less friendly context.

Make the change contingent on something you offer back or framed as meeting both parties' needs.

selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The offer is overly rigid and extractive—demanding "all" one scarce resource—so it misses obvious integrative possibilities (e.g., trading some water plus other concessions). selected_turns.text: I can do that. Would you also be able to give me one firewood?
selected_turns.why_important: This turn followed the opponent's explicit, transactional offer and immediately expanded the speaker's claim. That move turned the exchange into a stronger zero-sum negotiation early on and set a tone of claiming extra value rather than jointly creating value. It therefore materially affected the framing and direction of the whole interaction.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although it succeeded (the opponent later agreed), it is improvable because accepting then immediately asking for more risks appearing opportunistic and can erode trust or prompt retaliation in other contexts. It missed a chance to convert the opponent's transactional stance into a more integrative exchange (e.g., by proposing a mutually beneficial trade or offering a concession).
selected_turns.better_direction: Instead of immediately adding a new demand on top of acceptance, frame the follow-up as conditional or reciprocal (e.g., propose a small concession of your own in exchange, or ask if they'd be willing and note what you can give back). Alternatively, confirm the original agreement first and then, if necessary, present the extra request framed as a low-cost ask or as part of a trade-off.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — making the ask conditional or offering something in return signals reciprocity and fairness.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — reducing the appearance of opportunism lowers friction and the chance of stand-offs in other negotiations.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — a more reciprocal framing preserves trust and rapport.

selected_turns.text: I would like to share 1 food, 1 water and 2 firewood with you. Can you accept the deal?

selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It is a direct, self-favoring offer made without first eliciting the opponent's priorities or searching for tradeoffs. selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The message mixes an accidental-apology, empathy, and a vague request. The apology and filler language dilute the leverage of a timely trade-opening; the request for "a bit of extra food" is underspecified (no quantities, no willingness to concede on other items) and doesn't probe the opponent's minimum needs for water. That leaves ambiguity that slows efficient deal-shaping and misses an opportunity to structure a clear, mutually beneficial proposal.

For example, confirm their water minimum and propose explicit trade ratios or alternatives rather than a vague "bit of extra food."

selected_turns.why_important: This is the first substantive counter-offer from YOU and sets the early tone. selected_turns.text: You'll definitely need some additional calories if you want to do extra hiking.  I will give you all the additional food if you give me all the additional firewood.
selected_turns.why_important: This is a clear all-or-nothing quid-pro-quo that shifts the interaction from a cooperative exchange into a competitive, zero-sum framing. It directly shapes the allocation (you demand all firewood in return for all food) and therefore strongly influences what deals are possible and how fair the outcome will appear.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The demand is rigid and unnecessarily extreme given integrative potential (each party values different items). It ignores partial trades or splits that could increase total joint value and risks harming trust and perceived fairness. Even though it secured agreement later, it forecloses milder, more balanced options and signals hard bargaining.
selected_turns.better_direction: Soften the demand into a trade that leaves room for compromise: propose a specific partial trade (e.g., some firewood for some food), ask what the other values most, or offer a split that leaves the other party with some firewood or additional concession elsewhere. Frame it as a proposal to find a fair swap rather than a take-it-or-leave-it demand.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase (by enabling more integrative, mutually beneficial splits)

selected_turns.better_direction: Keep the preference but pair it with an explicit willingness to trade or a question about the other's priorities—e.g., express why firewood matters, then invite what they value most or suggest potential swaps. This frames the preference constructively and opens integrative bargaining.

selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It comes across as opportunistic because the partner had just accepted a cooperative split; taking the extra water without offering anything in return can reduce perceived fairness and relational goodwill. Even if the partner concedes, this type of move can lower trust and make future negotiations harder.

Framing the request as a favor and offering compensation preserves fairness.

selected_turns.text: My spouse calls me a water buffalo so I'm pretty water dependent if you couldn't tell.

selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The turn needlessly escalated scarcity over water instead of reciprocating the opponent's concession. Because it came right after their prosocial disclosure, it missed a chance to lock in a mutually beneficial trade framed around complementary priorities.
selected_turns.better_direction: Reciprocate the opponent's concession: emphasize appreciation for them taking on water needs or state your preference order (firewood highest) in a way that leaves room for trade-offs (e.g., 'I care most about firewood but also need some food — can we split water as you suggested?'). That would preserve integrative framing.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (acknowledges opponent's sacrifice)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase (reduces potential conflict)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase (more trust and goodwill)

selected_turns.text: Yes, I agree.  To confirm, you will get 2 waters, 2 food, and no firewood.  I will get 1 water, 1 food, and 3 firewood.  Thank you!
selected_turns.why_important: This confirmation finalized the allocation in explicit terms and closes the negotiation. How you confirm shapes the lasting perception of fairness and the relationship going forward.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The confirmation is factual but frames the outcome as a self-favoring result. It misses an opportunity to recognize the opponent's concession or to soften the finalization with gratitude/reciprocity language that would boost perceived fairness and rapport.
selected_turns.better_direction: When confirming, explicitly thank the opponent for their flexibility and acknowledge the trade they made (e.g., note their water need and appreciation for taking care of it). Optionally offer a small, symbolic concession or future reciprocity to balance perceptions (no need to change the split materially).

expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (perceived fairness improves with acknowledgment)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: neutral to increase (confirmation remains smooth but more positive)
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase (greater trust and goodwill for future interactions)

selected_turns.text: That's good.  Best way to stay healthy is with water.  How about you take 1 water and all the firewood?
selected_turns.why_important: This turn directly converted the opponent's expressed need for water into a concrete, self-favoring allocation that constrained the opponent's water despite their stated water dependency. It shifted the negotiation back toward a zero-sum split and determined the substantive terms other turns responded to.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It's competitive and somewhat tone-deaf: the opponent had just emphasized water importance, yet the offer limits their water. That both risks escalating tension and misses an opportunity to craft a fair integrative trade (e.g., trading firewood for sufficient water). The move is consequential but suboptimal for fairness and relationship.
selected_turns.better_direction: Pause and ask a brief question to clarify their minimum water need or propose a more balanced trade: e.g., explicitly offer all firewood in exchange for an amount of water that meets their stated need (or suggest splitting other items to compensate). Use an empathic framing ("I hear water is important — how much do you need?") or propose a concrete split that shows you're trading firewood for an appropriate share of water.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — moving to an explicit trade or eliciting needs opens integrative possibilities and reduces the chance of inefficient hard positions.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — asking for their minimum or offering a balanced trade would produce a more equitable allocation given stated needs.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — clarifying needs and offering a clear trade reduces misunderstandings and avoids counter-proposals that prolong negotiation.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — an empathic question or fair trade signals respect for the other's priorities and reduces friction.

selected_turns.text: Perfect!  I'm a northerner, so the firewood is all yours!  I'll need lot's of water to keep hydrated after the hike, and I'll probably be pretty hungry too.
selected_turns.why_important: This is an early cooperative concession that opened the door to a trade (you conceded firewood and revealed your priorities). It set the tone for an integrative split and could have anchored a fair, efficient allocation if executed slightly differently.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although cooperative, it's vague: it concedes firewood but doesn't quantify the trade (how much water/food you're asking for in return) or elicit the opponent's secondary needs. That left room for later misalignment and allowed the opponent to make a fully self-favoring proposal that you later accepted.
selected_turns.better_direction: Follow the concession with an explicit, conditional proposal or a clarifying question: e.g., state the allocation you're seeking in return ("I'm happy to give firewood if I can have X water and Y food") or ask what they'd need beyond firewood. Framing the concession as a trade ("I'll take water if you take firewood — how much water would you be comfortable giving?") would steer toward a clearer deal.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — explicit trade proposals reduce guesswork and help find Pareto-improving splits faster.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — making your needs explicit encourages reciprocal balancing rather than unilateral concession then passive acceptance.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — a concrete conditional offer accelerates convergence and reduces back-and-forth.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: increase — clear, reciprocal proposals signal cooperative intent and respect for both sides' needs.

selected_turns.text: How about I take 2 firewood but you can have 2 waters? Water is pretty important and I have a little extra.
selected_turns.why_important: This turn shifts the negotiation away from the opponent's cooperative proposal to split firewood and proposes a self-favoring allocation. It reframes the exchange into a competitive, resource-grabbing move at a moment when the other party had just proposed a fair split, so it strongly affects fairness perceptions and the tone of cooperation.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although it produced agreement, the move missed an opportunity to build on the opponent's prosocial framing to create a clearly integrative, mutually beneficial package. As phrased, it emphasizes taking the speaker's top priority rather than explicitly balancing tradeoffs or checking the opponent's priorities for water vs. other items. That risks appearing opportunistic and could have reduced trust or left potential value-creating trades unexplored.
selected_turns.better_direction: Accept or acknowledge the opponent's cooperative split first and then propose tradeoffs or ask a confirmatory question (e.g., confirm whether water is acceptable or whether they would prefer food) so the offer reads as collaborative problem-solving rather than self-favoring demand. Frame the proposal as a trade that helps both parties rather than primarily securing your top item.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (less appearance of opportunism, clearer reciprocal concession)

It's an early escalation that sets up a zero-sum framing around firewood, which determines much of the subsequent division dynamic.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Responding to the opponent's statement of need with another demand increased competition instead of exploring sharing or trades. At this stage a more cooperative or information-seeking response could have preserved integrative options (e.g., proposing a split, offering a trade, or asking about their absolute need), reducing conflict and enabling a better joint outcome.
selected_turns.better_direction: Shift from a direct demand to an information-seeking or cooperative move: acknowledge their need, propose split options, or ask what would be acceptable. Even if you need firewood, presenting it as a mutual problem to solve signals willingness to trade and avoids locking both parties into a win-lose fight over one resource.

selected_turns.text: I’m sorry to hear that! If there are 3 packages of food please take 2 of them. I could stand to eat less!

Without that, small misunderstandings about quantities or execution could lead to implementation friction or perceived imbalance.
selected_turns.better_direction: After offering the concession, explicitly confirm counts and logistics (e.g., 'Are there indeed 3 packages?' or 'Does that work for you if I take X and you take Y?') and express appreciation for the compromise so both sides clearly understand and feel good about the deal.

selected_turns.text: So could I. How about we split the firewood?
selected_turns.why_important: This turn de-escalates a zero-sum claim over firewood and opens a cooperative frame. It sets the negotiation's trajectory toward sharing and enables later tradeoffs (which ultimately produced agreement). Because it came early on the contested resource, it shaped subsequent offers and concessions.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The proposal is vague — "split the firewood" reduces tension but doesn't specify amounts or tradeoffs. That ambiguity can slow execution, leave room for misunderstanding, or miss chances to create more joint value (for example by explicitly linking the split to concessions on water or food). It's a good cooperative move but underspecified and thus improvable.
selected_turns.better_direction: Be concrete and integrative: propose specific numbers (e.g., "let's split: 2 and 2"), or propose the split plus an explicit trade ("I'll take X firewood if you take Y water / I can give you…"). Tie the split to the other's need (acknowledge their cold) and invite their preference for exact counts to speed agreement.

selected_turns.why_important: This turn marks a clear shift into competitive, zero-sum bargaining from the agent: after the opponent demanded all water, the agent immediately demands all the food. That mutual hardening strongly shapes the rest of the negotiation (it frames resources as up-for-grabs rather than opportunities for tradeoffs) and determines the final division.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It's harmful because it reciprocates the opponent's demanding stance rather than defusing it or exploring integrative trades. Demanding the entire food bundle simultaneously reduces options for package deals, damages perceived fairness, and risks escalation or stalled agreement in less cooperative settings.

selected_turns.why_important: This is a clear deal-shaping turn: it converts the other's insistence (they demanded at least 2 food) into a firm allocation that gives them their minimum food but explicitly denies any firewood. It narrows available outcomes and largely determines the distribution of scarce items.

selected_turns.text: I think I will need all the firewood since you are taking 2 foods. I will need to hunt and cook food from the land meaning I need my fire going all the time.
selected_turns.why_important: This is an escalation point: in direct response to the opponent's plea for one firewood, the agent counters by claiming 'all the firewood' and justifying it. That solidifies a competitive, zero-sum framing and raises the emotional/claim stakes of the negotiation.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: It's rigid and defensive—claiming 'all' firewood in reply to a request closes off simple, low-cost concessions that could have preserved joint value and goodwill. The justificatory tone primes the opponent to dig in or retaliate rather than look for tradeoffs. The move increases conflict while offering no compensating integrative option.
selected_turns.better_direction: Shift from absolute claim to problem-solving: acknowledge the opponent's need, state your own requirement succinctly, and propose a specific compromise or an exchange (for example, offer a share of firewood in exchange for fewer foods or provision of water). Avoid absolutist language like 'all' and instead invite a small trade or schedule (e.g., split or rotate use) so both sides feel heard.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase (by moving away from winner-take-all claims)

expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve (less antagonistic language will reduce resentment)

selected_turns.text: I really need food too, but I am willing to deal and let my kids go hungry I guess ☹️ I propose I get one food, 3 firewood and 1 water.
selected_turns.why_important: This is the agent's first concrete allocation proposal and therefore anchors expectations and the negotiation trajectory. It signals willingness to compromise and shapes what the opponent considers acceptable.
selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The offer is overly concessional regarding food (the agent gives up much of their own stated priority) and framed with self-deprecating/emotional language that weakens bargaining power and may encourage further demands. It also doesn't propose explicit tradeoffs or conditional concessions that would make a mutually beneficial split more likely.
selected_turns.better_direction: Make the opening offer firmer and more structured: claim a reasonable share of high-priority items, avoid self-demeaning language, and present conditional trades (e.g., 'I'm willing to give you X food if you give me Y firewood') or ask targeted questions about which items the opponent values less. Framing concessions as conditional will preserve leverage while enabling integrative deals.

selected_turns.text: I need a lot of food today. I was advised to eat at least 4 times a day so i may need all the food packages.

selected_turns.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: The move is overly absolutist and persuasive without offering tradeoffs or acknowledging the other's needs. That black-or-white claim likely provoked the opponent's defensive responses and reduced opportunities for integrative trades (e.g., trading food for firewood or water). It's consequential but clearly improvable.
selected_turns.better_direction: State that food is your top priority but avoid claiming 'all' items; acknowledge the opponent's needs and invite a trade or joint solution (e.g., 'Food is most important to me — could we split food but trade for firewood/water?'). Frame your need as high priority while keeping openness to exchange.

That choice missed a chance to convert their self-interested offer into a package deal that could satisfy both sides.
